question_text: The Moon's gravitational field strength is about 1.6 N/kg, compared to Earth's 10 N/kg. Explain why an astronaut weighs less on the Moon than on Earth, even though their mass stays the same.
correct_answer: Weight depends on the gravitational field strength, not mass. Since the Moon has a weaker gravitational field (1.6 N/kg), it pulls with less force on the astronaut's mass. Weight = mass × gravitational field strength, so a weaker field produces less weight.
explanation: Weight is calculated using W = m × g. Mass (m) never changes, but gravitational field strength (g) is weaker on the Moon. Therefore weight is directly proportional to gravitational field strength—a weaker field means less weight.
